narrative:
Markets are holding in a business-as-usual regime this morning, with a stress score of 26 anchoring expectations at the cautious end of normal. The five-day decline in SPY of 0.7 percent sits well within routine volatility, and nothing in the positioning or pricing framework suggests systemic concern. This regime persists because credit is calm, equity breadth remains broad, and equities have absorbed recent weakness without panic.

The VIX at 17.1 sits at the 38th percentile of its decade-long distribution, indicating a fairly mundane volatility environment with no tail compression or elevation. The term structure remains in contango, the normal shape, with no signal of near-term stress. High-yield spreads at 272 basis points trade at the 8th percentile tightly, while investment-grade spreads at 73 basis points are similarly compressed, both with negative z-scores indicating credit is pricing stability and even benignity. This credit behavior strongly confirms the equities story rather than contradicting it.

China-focused equities and materials are the week's visible drags, with FXI down 3.6 percent and the materials sector off 3.2 percent, while industrials lag at 2.3 percent lower. Conversely, Germany and UK equities are outperforming with gains of 1.9 and 1.5 percent respectively, and energy has rallied 1.8 percent. These moves suggest rotation rather than broad capitulation, and with only two sectors red out of eleven and two emerging markets lagging out of eight, the breadth backdrop remains genuinely healthy.
